items.q: What is Taylor's theory of scientific management?
items.correct: Workers are motivated primarily by money; efficiency is maximised by breaking tasks into standardised steps, training workers in 'the one best way', and using piece-rate pay.
items.misconception: Taylor said workers are motivated by recognition and teamwork.
items.random_wrong: Taylor invented the assembly line at Ford.
items.why: Students confuse Taylor with Mayo or Maslow. Taylor's view was that workers respond to pay incentives, not social or psychological factors — his theory is famously narrow and mechanistic.

items.q: What does Maslow's hierarchy of needs propose about motivation?
items.correct: Humans are motivated by a hierarchy of needs (physiological, safety, social, esteem, self-actualisation) — lower needs must be substantially met before higher ones motivate.
items.misconception: Maslow said money is the most important motivator at work.
items.random_wrong: Maslow said employees must be promoted every year.
items.why: Students conflate Maslow with Taylor (who DID emphasise pay). Maslow's whole point is that once basic needs are met, higher needs (recognition, self-actualisation) motivate — not more money.

items.correct: Competitive rivalry, threat of new entrants, threat of substitutes, bargaining power of suppliers, bargaining power of buyers — together determining industry attractiveness and potential profitability.
items.misconception: Porter's Five Forces are five business strategies a firm should choose between.
items.random_wrong: Porter's Five Forces are five physical security measures for a factory.
items.why: Five Forces analyse INDUSTRY structure, not firm strategy choices (that's Porter's generic strategies, a different framework). Students conflate the two Porter models.

items.q: Resistance from missing information is best treated with:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Education and communication. “Coercion” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: Last resort with high cost to trust
items.why: It's Last resort with high cost to trust. “First choice” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Number of resistance sources Kotter & Schlesinger list?
items.why: Exact answer: 6. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
